content.assessment: Admissions-only reform is framed by the minister herself as the primary lever under consideration, giving it a degree of institutional legitimacy that structural alternatives lack. Yet the evidence shows that even this narrower framing has already been politically weaponized as equivalent to full structural transformation, suggesting the coalition-preserving intent of this design does not guarantee reduced opposition intensity in practice.
opportunities: Framing as administrative/technical rather than structural could allow the government to build a narrower, more defensible coalition focused on process fairness rather than track abolition
strengths: Aligns with the minister's own stated preferred scope, suggesting some governing-coalition support exists for this pathway [F-live-political_feasibility-04]
Avoids directly challenging the founding church/liberal coalition since track structures remain intact [F-live-political_feasibility-01]
threats: Persistent risk that any visible change to admissions is reinterpreted by opposition through the same communism-era framing applied to broader reforms, eroding the political safety this design was intended to provide [F-live-political_feasibility-04]
weaknesses: Opposition has already reframed even this narrower review as disguised structural transformation, showing that scope-limitation does not reliably reduce opposition intensity or rhetorical escalation [F-live-political_feasibility-04]
